@graph.name: Land Rover Defender L316 Core Variants

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Defender L316 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned L316 engine typically costs £1,800–£7,000 supply only, depending on engine — the Td5 sits at £2,500–£4,500, while the 2.2 Td4 Puma runs £3,200–£5,800. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Land Rover Defender L316 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 300Tdi (1990–1998) — a mechanically injected, indirect-injection turbo-diesel with no ECU, no DPF, no EGR. It's the last of the truly bulletproof Land Rover diesels and has a documented reputation for exceeding 300,000 miles with routine servicing.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Defender L316?

acceptedAnswer.text: Td5 injector harness oil contamination, Tdi injection pump wear, Puma-era dual‑mass flywheel failure, and — unrelated to the engine — chassis corrosion on the rear crossmember and outriggers. Each issue has a clear preventative fix.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Defender L316 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine and which era. Pre‑Puma diesels (200Tdi, 300Tdi, Td5) are generally very reliable with proper maintenance. Puma-era 2.4 and 2.2 Td4 units are more refined but introduce common‑rail complexity and DMF/emissions‑system risks that the earlier engines never faced.

mainEntity.name: Which Land Rover Defender L316 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a Puma‑era 2.4 or 2.2 Td4 without documented DMF, clutch and injector history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A neglected Td5 with oil‑contaminated ECU wiring is a close second.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my Defender L316 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Blue smoke, rough idle, persistent loss of power, or a Td5 ECU that’s been oil‑soaked are key warning signs. For Tdi engines, hard starting when hot points to injection pump wear. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Land Rover Defender L316 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the chassis. Against a well‑kept £15,000 Defender, a £3,500 Td5 recon is ~23% of the car’s value — safe. Against a sub‑£8,000 example with significant chassis rot, the same recon brushes our 60% Land Rover threshold — walk away and re‑chassis first. Always value the chassis before the engine.

mainEntity.name: How long do Land Rover Defender L316 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 300Tdi and 200Tdi can exceed 300,000 miles with proper servicing. The Td5 is similarly durable, with its injector harness as the main consumable. Puma‑era units are limited by DMF and injector longevity — addressed proactively, they can also reach 150,000+ miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the Defender L316 and the new Defender L663?

acceptedAnswer.text: The L316 is the original coil‑spring Defender (1990–2016) with ladder‑frame chassis, solid axles and a simple utilitarian interior. The L663 (2019–present) is a unibody SUV with independent suspension, advanced electronics and a completely different engine lineup. Costs and repair approaches are not interchangeable — see the L663 Generation Page for that model.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Defender L316 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and in vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible on the driver’s side of the engine block on Tdi/Td5 units, and on the cylinder head area on Puma engines. Tell us your variant and approximate year (pre‑1998, 1998–2006, or 2007–2016) and we’ll identify it — 12 vetted Land Rover specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Land Rover Defender L316 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the original Defender L316 — from the 2.5-litre 200Tdi to the Puma-era 2.4 and 2.2 Td4 units. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the Td5-to-Puma switch that defined this generation's final decade. Backed by 292+ real UK L316 enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Land Rover Defender L316 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every diesel and petrol engine fitted to the original Defender L316 — from the indirect-injection 2.5-litre to the Puma-era common-rail Td4 units.

overview.intro: The L316 is the original coil-spring Defender, produced from 1983 to 2016 and officially branded as the "Defender" from 1990 onwards. It succeeded the leaf-sprung Series III and introduced a longer wheelbase, coil-spring suspension, and — across its 33-year production run — a succession of diesel engines from the 2.5-litre naturally aspirated unit through the 200Tdi, 300Tdi, Td5 and finally the Puma-era 2.4 and 2.2 Td4 common-rail diesels. A significant 2007 update brought the Ford-sourced 2.4-litre Puma engine, quieter interior and redesigned dashboard, splitting this generation into two distinct mechanical eras.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Land Rover L316 (coil-spring, ladder-frame chassis) • Predecessor: Land Rover Series III (leaf-sprung) • Successor: Land Rover Defender L663 (2019–present) • Sibling body styles in this generation: Defender 90 (short wheelbase), Defender 110 (long wheelbase), Defender 130 (extended)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The L316 Defender generated 292 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED], concentrated on the Td5 and Puma-era diesel units as the original Defender parc ages out of daily use.

items.quote: The 300Tdi is the last of the mechanically injected Land Rover diesels — no electronics, no common-rail complexity, just a simple, tough, indirect-injection turbo-diesel that will run on almost any diesel fuel. It's widely considered the most dependable engine ever fitted to a Defender.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want off-grid reliability and simplicity.

items.quote: The Rover V8 delivers the only petrol soundtrack the original Defender ever offered with genuine character. Parts availability remains strong thanks to the MG/Rover aftermarket. Fuel consumption is the real cost — expect single-digit mpg in real-world use.
items.whoItsFor: Enthusiasts who want V8 noise over diesel economy.

items.quote: The Puma-era 2.4-litre common-rail diesel was the first Defender engine with significant electronic complexity. Dual-mass flywheel failure, EGR valve clogging, and injector issues are well-documented weak points that earlier mechanical engines never suffered.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want a more refined Defender and are prepared for modern diesel maintenance costs.

items.quote: The 2.2 Puma shares the 2.4's common-rail complexity but adds AdBlue/DEF system complexity on late models. DPF, EGR and injector failures can run into thousands — repairs that the Tdi and Td5 simply never face.
items.whoItsFor: Late-model Defender buyers with deeper pockets and full service history.

items.quote: Mechanically simple, no ECU, no DPF, no EGR — just a tough, proven diesel that will outlast the chassis it sits in. Parts are cheap and every Land Rover specialist knows them inside out.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want Defender capability without modern diesel complexity.

items.quote: The Td5 is often dismissed as 'the complicated one' between the Tdi and the Puma, but it's actually a sweet spot — electronic injection but no DPF, no EGR, no AdBlue. The 5-cylinder character and 122 bhp make it the best-driving of the pre-Puma diesels.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want a refined-but-simple Defender daily driver.

ownershipEconomics.h2: Land Rover Defender L316 — Ownership Economics

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Diesel — the Defender's most dependable engine
Best Value — refined, no DPF, strong parts supply
Risk vs Reward — refined but electronically complex
Most Expensive — modern diesel costs on a classic chassis
Enthusiast Only — glorious sound, brutal fuel costs

economicsRule.title: The L316 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200–£5,800 Td4 recon against a £10,000–£25,000 Defender sits at 13–58% of the car's value — a repair decision, not a financial one. Against a sub-£8,000 high-mileage example, the same recon brushes our 60% Land Rover threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Value the vehicle first, then the engine.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L316 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 300Tdi — simple mechanics, no electronics, cheap parts.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L316 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 2.2 Td4 (Puma) — DPF, EGR, AdBlue and common-rail injectors add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L316 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: Td5 (post-2002) — 5-cylinder refinement without the Puma's emissions-system complexity.

problems.title: Td5 Injector Wiring Harness Oil Contamination
problems.rootCause: The Td5's injector wiring harness runs inside the rocker cover and is prone to oil ingress through the ECU connector. Engine oil wicks up the harness and contaminates the ECU, causing misfires, rough running, and eventually complete failure.
problems.recommendation: Check the ECU connector for oil at every service — a £200 harness replacement is dramatically cheaper than the £800+ ECU replacement that follows if ignored. This is the single most common Td5 failure and the easiest to prevent.

cta.label: Compare Td5 engine prices for your Defender L316 →

problems.title: 300Tdi / 200Tdi Injection Pump Wear
problems.affectedModels: Defender L316 200Tdi and 300Tdi (11 L / 16 L / 23 L, 1990–1998).
problems.rootCause: The Bosch VE mechanical injection pump wears internally over high mileage, leading to poor starting, reduced power, and eventually complete pump failure. The 300Tdi's electronic fuel shut-off solenoid can also fail, leaving the engine unable to stop.
problems.recommendation: If your Tdi is hard to start when hot or feels down on power, have the injection pump timing and delivery checked immediately — a £300–£600 rebuild is far cheaper than the £700+ replacement that follows total failure.

cta.label: Compare 300Tdi engine prices for your Defender L316 →

problems.title: 2.4 / 2.2 Td4 Dual-Mass Flywheel (DMF) Failure
problems.rootCause: The Puma-era Defenders use a dual-mass flywheel to dampen drivetrain vibration from the common-rail diesel. The DMF wears over time, causing rattle on idle, judder on take-off, and eventually complete failure that can damage the starter motor and gearbox input shaft.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of rattle on idle (especially when the clutch is depressed), have the DMF inspected immediately — a £600–£1,000 preventative clutch-and-DMF job is far cheaper than the £1,200+ repair that follows if the DMF fails catastrophically.

cta.label: Compare Td4 engine prices for your Defender L316 →

problems.title: Defender L316 Chassis Corrosion
problems.affectedModels: All Defender L316 variants, particularly 1990–2006 examples.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but the single biggest reason L316 Defenders fail MOTs and get scrapped. The ladder-frame chassis traps moisture and mud, rotting from the inside out — rear crossmember, outriggers, and bulkhead footwells are the primary failure points.
problems.recommendation: Have the chassis inspected on any L316 over 15 years old — it's the single most common reason an otherwise sound Defender is written off. A £300–£800 preventative treatment is trivial against the £2,000–£4,500 chassis replacement that follows neglect.
